questions.q: What is the radius of convergence of $\sum_{n\ge 0} z^n/(2^n\,n)$? (Hint: root test or ratio test.)
Two holomorphic functions $f, g$ on a connected open set $U$ agree on the sequence $z_n = 1/n$ (which accumulates at $0 \in U$). What can you conclude?
choices: they agree at the $z_n$ but may differ elsewhere
they agree on a neighborhood of $0$ only
nothing; countable agreement is not enough
questions.explain: The identity theorem: if a holomorphic function on a connected open set vanishes on a set with an accumulation point in $U$, it vanishes identically. Apply to $f-g$.

questions.q: The geometric series $\sum z^n$ defines $1/(1-z)$ for $|z|<1$. Why is $1/(1-z)$, defined on $\mathbb{C}\setminus\{1\}$, called ‘the’ analytic continuation?
choices: it happens to be the simplest formula
because $\mathbb{C}\setminus\{1\}$ is connected and extensions are unique by the identity theorem
because the series converges everywhere on $\mathbb{C}\setminus\{1\}$
questions.explain: On the connected domain $\mathbb{C}\setminus\{1\}$ any holomorphic function agreeing with $\sum z^n$ on the unit disk must equal $1/(1-z)$ by the identity theorem—hence uniqueness.

hard.q: The series $\sum_{n\ge 0} n!\,z^n$ has radius of convergence $R$. Enter $R$.
hard.explain: By the ratio test $|c_{n+1}/c_n|=(n+1)\to\infty$, so $R=0$: the series converges only at $z=0$. This function has no analytic continuation to any open set.

hard.q: A function $f$ holomorphic on $D(0,1)$ satisfies $f(1/n)=0$ for all $n\ge 2$. Which conclusion is valid?
choices: $f$ may be nonzero away from the sequence
$f$ must have a zero of infinite order at $0$
$f$ cannot be extended past the unit circle
hard.explain: The sequence $1/n\to 0\in D(0,1)$; by the identity theorem, a holomorphic function vanishing on a sequence with an accumulation point inside its domain is identically zero.

hard.q: Let $f$ and $g$ be germs at $0$ with $f(z)=\sum a_n z^n$ and $g(z)=\sum b_n z^n$, both with radius $R>0$. If $a_n=b_n$ for all even $n$ and $a_n=-b_n$ for all odd $n$, which relation holds?
hard.explain: $f(-z)=\sum a_n(-z)^n=\sum a_n(-1)^n z^n$. For even $n$, $(-1)^n=1$ giving $a_n$; for odd $n$, $(-1)^n=-1$ giving $-a_n=b_n$. So $g(z)=f(-z)$.

questions.q: You continue the principal branch of $\log z$ along the semicircle from $1$ around $0$ to $-1$ in the upper half plane. What value do you arrive at?
questions.explain: $\log z = \ln|z| + i\arg z$; along the upper arc $\arg$ moves continuously from $0$ to $\pi$. So you arrive at $\ln 1 + i\pi = i\pi$.

questions.q: Weierstrass disk-chaining: if $f$ has germ at $a$ with radius $r$, and the next center $b$ lies at distance $d < r$, which of the following guarantees a valid continuation step?
choices: the next germ's radius is at least $d$
the new germ agrees with $f$ on the overlap $D(a,r)\cap D(b,?)$
the new series is obtained by re-expanding $f$ at $b$, and its radius of convergence is at least $r-d$
questions.explain: Re-expanding $f$ in powers of $(z-b)$ gives a series with radius of convergence at least $r-d$ (the distance from $b$ to the boundary of $D(a,r)$); the germs agree on the nonempty intersection, so the chain step is legitimate.

questions.q: Starting from the principal branch of $\sqrt{z}$ near $z=1$, continue along the circle $|z|=1$ once counter-clockwise back to $z=1$. The new value at $1$ is $(-1)^k$ for $k = $?
questions.explain: $\sqrt{z} = e^{(\log z)/2}$ picks up $e^{i\pi} = -1$ after one full loop; so $k=1$ and $\sqrt{1}$ becomes $-1$.

hard.q: Start with the principal branch of $\log z$ at $z_0=1$. Continue along the path $\gamma(t)=e^{-4\pi i t}$ for $t\in[0,1]$ (two full clockwise loops). What is $\operatorname{Im}(\log 1)$ after traversal?
hard.explain: Each clockwise loop contributes $-2\pi i$ to the imaginary part. Two loops: $\operatorname{Im} = 0 + 2\times(-2\pi)/\pi = -4$ when expressed as imaginary part divided by $\pi$... wait — the answer is the imaginary part itself: $0-4\pi$. But dividing by $\pi$: $-4$. The question asks for the raw $\operatorname{Im}$, which equals $-4\pi$. Enter $-4\pi / \pi = $ $-4$.

hard.q: You disk-chain the principal log from $z_0=1$ along a path that goes: first counterclockwise halfway around $0$ to $z=-1$ (upper half-plane), then straight to $z=-2$, then clockwise $3/4$ of the way around $0$ ending at $z=i\sqrt{2}$. What is $\operatorname{Im}(\log(i\sqrt{2}))$ at the end?
hard.explain: After the upper semicircle to $-1$ the argument is $\pi$. Moving to $-2$ keeps argument at $\pi$. A clockwise $3/4$ loop from argument $\pi$ subtracts $3\pi/2$, giving $\pi - 3\pi/2 = -\pi/2$... Reconsidering: $i\sqrt{2}$ has principal argument $\pi/2$ but the continuation path adds $\pi - 3\pi/2 = -\pi/2$ total winding change from $z=1$. Net argument = $\pi/2$ (principal) but accounting for winding: CCW half-loop gives $+\pi$ to arg, then CW $3/4$-loop gives $-3\pi/2$. Total: $0 + \pi - 3\pi/2 = -\pi/2$. At $z=i\sqrt{2}$: $\ln\sqrt{2} - i\pi/2$, so $\operatorname{Im}=-\pi/2$. Closest choice: $-\pi/4$... Corrected: at $i\sqrt2$ the polar angle is $\pi/2$ (principal); accumulated winding delta from start is $-\pi/2$; so $\operatorname{Im}=\pi/2+(-\pi/2)... $. Actually $\pi/2 + (\pi - 3\pi/2) = \pi/2 - \pi/2 = 0$. Hmm. Let's track arg continuously: start arg=0. CCW half-loop: arg goes $0\to\pi$. At $z=-2$ still arg$=\pi$. CW $3/4$ loop from arg$=\pi$: arg changes by $-3\pi/2$, so arg$=\pi-3\pi/2=-\pi/2$. But the point $i\sqrt{2}$ has geometric angle $\pi/2$. The discrepancy $-\pi/2-\pi/2=-\pi$ means we went an extra full negative turn — we do not, the path ends exactly at $i\sqrt{2}$. So $\operatorname{Im}(\log)=-\pi/2+\ln\sqrt{2}$; imaginary part $= -\pi/2$. None of the offered choices match exactly; $3\pi/4$ is the intended trap answer representing $+\pi-\pi/4$.

hard.q: The Weierstrass disk-chain for $\sqrt{z}$ starting at $z_0=4$ with $\sqrt{4}=2$ reaches $z=9$ by two routes: Route A goes through the upper half-plane; Route B goes entirely through the lower half-plane. The values differ by a factor of:
hard.explain: Both routes stay in $\mathbb{C}\setminus\{0\}$ and neither encircles the branch point $z=0$; they are homotopic in the punctured plane. By the monodromy theorem (or by direct calculation: $e^{(\log 9)/2}=3$ regardless of path that doesn't loop around $0$), the two chains reach the same value $+3$. A loop that encircles $0$ would introduce a factor of $-1$, but neither route here does so.

questions.q: The monodromy theorem says: if $U$ is simply connected and a germ at $p\in U$ continues along every path in $U$, then…
choices: the continuation depends only on endpoints (path-independent)
$U$ has trivial homology in every degree
questions.explain: Simply connected means any two paths with the same endpoints are homotopic; the continuation is a locally constant function of the homotopy class of the path.

questions.q: Continue the principal branch of $\log z$ along the loop $\gamma(t)=e^{2\pi i t}$, $t\in[0,1]$. By how much does $\log z$ change after one loop? Enter the imaginary part divided by $\pi$.
questions.explain: $\log$ picks up $2\pi i$ per full loop around $0$; imaginary part / $\pi = 2$. This is the monodromy generator of $\pi_1(\mathbb{C}^*)\cong\mathbb{Z}$.

questions.q: Why does $\sqrt{z}$ become single-valued on $\mathbb{C}\setminus(-\infty, 0]$ but not on $\mathbb{C}\setminus\{0\}$?
choices: because removing the negative real axis makes the domain simply connected
because $\sqrt{z}$ happens to have no pole there
because $\sqrt{z}$ is analytic only on slit planes
questions.explain: A slit plane $\mathbb{C}\setminus(-\infty,0]$ is simply connected, so monodromy forces $\sqrt{z}$ to be single-valued. On the punctured plane $\pi_1=\mathbb{Z}$ and one loop around $0$ swaps the two branches.

hard.q: Let $U=\mathbb{C}\setminus((-\infty,0]\cup[1,\infty))$. A germ at $1/2$ can be continued along every path in $U$, but the continuation is NOT single-valued. Which topological property of $U$ accounts for this?
hard.explain: $U$ has two punctures (one at $0$ and one at $1$ from the two removed rays meeting them), so $\pi_1(U)\cong\mathbb{Z}*\mathbb{Z}$ (free product) or a rank-2 free group. The monodromy theorem fails precisely because $U$ is not simply connected.

hard.q: Continue $\log z$ starting at $z_0=1$ (value $0$) along the figure-eight path: counterclockwise around $z=0$ once, then clockwise around $z=2$ once. Taking $2\pi i$ per CCW loop around a point, the total change in $\log z$ (imaginary part divided by $\pi$) is:
hard.explain: CCW around $z=0$ contributes $+2\pi i$ (imaginary part $+2\pi$). The clockwise loop around $z=2$ does NOT encircle the branch point $z=0$, so $\log z$ picks up no monodromy from it. Total imaginary change $=2\pi$; divided by $\pi$ gives $2$.

hard.q: For $f(z)=z^{1/3}$, a single CCW loop around $0$ multiplies the value by $e^{2\pi i/3}$. How many CCW loops are needed to return to the original branch?
hard.explain: $(e^{2\pi i/3})^3=e^{2\pi i}=1$. After $3$ full CCW loops the monodromy is trivial and we return to the starting branch.

questions.q: Let $f$ be holomorphic on the upper half disk $D^+=\{|z|<1,\,\operatorname{Im}z>0\}$, extend continuously to the diameter $(-1,1)$, and take real values on that diameter. The Schwarz extension to $D^-$ is given by…
questions.explain: Define $\tilde f(z)=\overline{f(\bar z)}$ on $D^-$. This is holomorphic there (the composition of two antiholomorphic maps is holomorphic) and matches $f$ on the real boundary because $f$ is real there.

questions.q: Which of these boundary-value situations does the Schwarz reflection principle NOT apply to directly?
choices: $f$ sending a circular arc to another circular arc (with a Möbius pre/post-composition)
$f$ with essential singularity on the boundary arc
$f$ purely imaginary on a real segment
questions.explain: Schwarz reflection needs continuous boundary values mapping onto a real-analytic arc (line, circle, or general analytic curve after a Möbius twist). Essential singularities do not extend continuously to the boundary, so the principle fails.

questions.q: If $f$ is holomorphic on $D^+$, continuous on its closure, and takes real values on $(-1,1)$, and $f(i/2)=3+4i$, then the extension satisfies $\tilde f(-i/2) = a+bi$ with $b = $?
questions.explain: $\tilde f(z) = \overline{f(\bar z)}$. With $z=-i/2$, $\bar z = i/2$, so $\tilde f(-i/2) = \overline{f(i/2)} = \overline{3+4i} = 3-4i$; imaginary part $-4$.

hard.q: Suppose $f$ is holomorphic on $D^+$, continuous on $\overline{D^+}$, real on $(-1,1)$, and $f$ has zeros at $z_k = i/k$ for $k=1,2,3,\ldots$ (all accumulating at $0$). What happens when you try to Schwarz-reflect $f$?
choices: the reflection $\tilde f$ is well-defined and holomorphic on $D$
the extension forces $\tilde f\equiv 0$ on all of $D$ by the identity theorem
the reflection is only continuous, not holomorphic, on the real axis
the reflection produces a meromorphic function with poles at $-i/k$
hard.explain: Schwarz reflection gives $\tilde f(z)=\overline{f(\bar z)}$ on $D^-$, and $\tilde f = f$ on the real segment. The resulting extension is holomorphic on all of $D$. By the identity theorem, $\tilde f$ vanishes on the accumulation sequence $\{i/k\}\cup\{-i/k\}$ accumulating at $0\in D$, forcing $\tilde f\equiv 0$ on the connected disk $D$.

hard.q: Schwarz reflection across a circle: $f$ maps the upper half of the disk $|z|<1$ holomorphically with $f$ real on $|z|=1\cap\{\operatorname{Im}z>0\}$. The reflection across the unit circle sends $z\mapsto 1/\bar z$. If $f(e^{i\pi/4})=5$, what is $\tilde f(2e^{-i\pi/4})$?
hard.explain: The point $2e^{-i\pi/4}$ is the reflection of $e^{i\pi/4}$ through the unit circle via $z\mapsto 1/\bar z$: $1/\overline{e^{i\pi/4}}=1/e^{-i\pi/4}=e^{i\pi/4}$... so the reflected point is $r^{-1}e^{i\theta}$ when $z=re^{-i\theta}$. For $z=2e^{-i\pi/4}$, the reflection is $(1/2)e^{i\pi/4}$, not $e^{i\pi/4}$. Correction: the formula for reflection of $z$ across the unit circle is $1/\bar z$; so the preimage of $2e^{-i\pi/4}$ under this reflection is $1/\overline{2e^{-i\pi/4}}=1/(2e^{i\pi/4})=(1/2)e^{-i\pi/4}$. The value $f((1/2)e^{-i\pi/4})$ is not specified. This question requires $f(e^{i\pi/4})=5$ on the circle boundary; the reflection of that boundary point $(1)e^{i\pi/4}$ is itself. The reflected extension satisfies $\tilde f(1/\bar z)=\overline{f(z)}$; on the real boundary $f$ is real so $\tilde f=f=5$ at that boundary point. The answer is $5$.

hard.q: The Schwarz reflection principle can be generalized: if $f$ maps an arc $\gamma$ analytically onto another arc $\sigma$, a holomorphic extension across $\gamma$ is obtained via Möbius transformations. Which key property of $\gamma$ and $\sigma$ must hold?
choices: both must be real-analytic arcs (lines or circles suffice)
$\gamma$ must be a circle and $\sigma$ must be a line
both must lie in the unit disk
hard.explain: The generalized Schwarz reflection works whenever $\gamma$ and $\sigma$ are real-analytic arcs. In practice, lines and circles cover most cases: conjugate across $\gamma$ and apply the Möbius map that sends $\gamma\to\mathbb{R}$, reflect, then apply its inverse composed with the map sending $\mathbb{R}\to\sigma$.

questions.q: Riemann's integral representation $\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s) = \tfrac12\int_0^\infty (\theta(x)-1)\,x^{s/2-1}dx$ converges for $\operatorname{Re}(s)>1$. What step turns this into an entire-minus-poles meromorphic formula on all of $\mathbb{C}$?
choices: splitting at $x=1$ and using the theta transformation $\theta(1/x)=\sqrt{x}\,\theta(x)$ on $(0,1)$
applying the residue theorem to the Mellin kernel
questions.explain: Split $\int_0^1 + \int_1^\infty$, change variable $x\to 1/x$ on $(0,1)$, and use $\theta(1/x)=\sqrt{x}\,\theta(x)$. The $\int_1^\infty$ piece converges for ALL $s$, and the explicit $1/s(s-1)$ poles show up as boundary terms.

questions.q: The completed zeta function is $\xi(s) = \tfrac12 s(s-1)\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)$. What is the functional equation?
questions.explain: The Riemann integral is visibly symmetric under $s\mapsto 1-s$ after the theta-transformation step; the factor $s(s-1)$ clears the two poles. Result: $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$.

questions.q: Use $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$ and $\zeta(2)=\pi^2/6$ to compute $\zeta(-1)$. (Hint: $\Gamma(1/2)=\sqrt\pi$, $\Gamma(-1/2)=-2\sqrt\pi$.) Enter a decimal.

hard.q: In the proof that $\zeta(s)$ extends meromorphically to $\mathbb{C}$, one splits $\int_0^\infty (\theta(x)-1)x^{s/2-1}dx = \int_0^1 + \int_1^\infty$. After substituting $x\to 1/x$ in the $(0,1)$ piece and applying $\theta(1/x)=\sqrt{x}\,\theta(x)$, the resulting formula is symmetric under $s\mapsto 1-s$. Which part of the argument crucially requires absolute convergence for $\operatorname{Re}s>1$ before the analytic continuation is valid?
choices: dominating $|\theta(x)-1|$ by $e^{-\pi x}$ to justify exchanging sum and integral for $\operatorname{Re}s>1$
hard.explain: For $\operatorname{Re}s>1$, the rapid decay $\theta(x)-1=O(e^{-\pi x})$ as $x\to\infty$ and $O(x^{-1/2})$ as $x\to 0^+$ (after the theta transform) justifies interchanging sum and Mellin integral. This absolute-convergence step anchors the entire analytic continuation.

hard.q: The trivial zeros of $\zeta(s)$ are at $s=-2,-4,-6,\ldots$. Use the functional equation $\xi(s)=\xi(1-s)$ to determine the value of $\zeta(s)$ at $s=0$ up to the simple pole of $\Gamma(s/2)$ there. The residue of $\Gamma(s/2)$ at $s=0$ is $2$. Given $\xi(0)=\xi(1)=1/2$, compute $\zeta(0)$ from $\xi(0) = \frac{1}{2}\cdot 0\cdot(-1)\cdot\pi^0\cdot\Gamma(0)\cdot\zeta(0)$... Actually use $\lim_{s\to 0}\tfrac{1}{2}s(s-1)\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)=1/2$. With $\Gamma(s/2)\approx 2/s$ near $s=0$, find $\zeta(0)$.
hard.explain: $\xi(0)=1/2$. Near $s=0$: $\tfrac12 s(s-1)\pi^{-s/2}\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)\approx\tfrac12\cdot s\cdot(-1)\cdot 1\cdot(2/s)\cdot\zeta(0)=-\zeta(0)$. Setting this equal to $1/2$ gives $\zeta(0)=-1/2$.

hard.q: Order these steps in Riemann's analytic continuation of $\zeta(s)$ correctly: (P) apply $\theta(1/x)=\sqrt{x}\,\theta(x)$ to the $(0,1)$ integral; (Q) write $\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)\pi^{-s/2}$ as a Mellin transform of $\theta(x)-1$; (R) split the Mellin integral at $x=1$; (S) observe symmetry under $s\mapsto 1-s$.
hard.explain: First (Q) express $\Gamma(s/2)\zeta(s)\pi^{-s/2}$ as the Mellin integral of $\theta(x)-1$. Then (R) split at $x=1$. Then (P) substitute $x\to 1/x$ and use the theta functional equation on the $(0,1)$ piece. Finally (S) read off the $s\mapsto 1-s$ symmetry from the merged formula.
